section.title: Saul Delivers Jabesh-gilead
section.sectionRationale: Saul's kingship is proved in action. Nahash the Ammonite besieges Jabesh-gilead and demands to gouge out every right eye. When the news reaches Saul at his plow, the Spirit of God rushes on him; he rallies all Israel, routs the Ammonites, and rescues the city. Saul refuses to take revenge on those who had doubted him, giving glory to the LORD who has worked salvation, and the kingship is joyfully renewed at Gilgal. It is one movement: the Spirit-empowered king delivers his people and is confirmed.
section.thesis: When Saul is moved by the Spirit of God, he becomes the deliverer Israel needed, saving Jabesh-gilead, crediting the victory to the LORD, and sparing his critics, so that at his best Saul shows what a king under God can be, a Spirit-filled saviour of his people, and points beyond himself to the King who saves for good.
section.frameNote: Four beats: Nahash's cruel siege of Jabesh, verses 1 to 4; the Spirit rushes on Saul and Israel rallies, verses 5 to 8; the rescue of Jabesh-gilead, verses 9 to 11; and mercy to critics and the kingship renewed, verses 12 to 15.

units.label: Nahash's cruel siege of Jabesh
units.note: that I may put out everyone's right eye

verses.text: Then Nahash the Ammonite came up and laid siege to Jabesh-gilead. All the men of Jabesh said to him, “Make a treaty with us, and we will serve you.”
But Nahash the Ammonite replied, “I will make a treaty with you on one condition, that I may put out everyone’s right eye and bring reproach upon all Israel.”
“Hold off for seven days,” replied the elders of Jabesh, “and let us send messengers throughout Israel. If there is no one to save us, we will surrender to you.”
When the messengers came to Gibeah of Saul and relayed these words in the hearing of the people, they all wept aloud.

units.label: The Spirit rushes on Saul and Israel rallies
units.note: the Spirit of God rushed upon him

verses.text: Just then Saul was returning from the field, behind his oxen. “What troubles the people?” asked Saul. “Why are they weeping?” And they relayed to him the words of the men from Jabesh.
When Saul heard their words, the {{ruach|Spirit of God}} rushed upon him, and he burned with great anger.

He took a pair of oxen, cut them into pieces, and sent them by messengers throughout the land of Israel, proclaiming, “This is what will be done to the oxen of anyone who does not march behind Saul and Samuel.” Then the {{pachad|terror of the LORD}} fell upon the people, and they came out together as one man.
And when Saul numbered them at Bezek, there were 300,000 Israelites and 30,000 men of Judah.

So they said to the messengers who had come, “Tell the men of Jabesh-gilead: ‘Deliverance will be yours tomorrow by the time the sun is hot.’” And when the messengers relayed this to the men of Jabesh, they rejoiced.
Then the men of Jabesh said to Nahash, “Tomorrow we will come out, and you can do with us whatever seems good to you.”

The next day Saul organized the troops into three divisions, and during the morning watch they invaded the camp of the Ammonites and slaughtered them, until the hottest part of the day. And the survivors were so scattered that no two of them were left together.

units.label: Mercy to critics and the kingship renewed
units.note: today the LORD has worked salvation in Israel

verses.text: Then the people said to Samuel, “Who said that Saul should not reign over us? Bring those men here so we can kill them!”
But Saul ordered, “No one shall be put to death this day, for today the LORD has worked {{teshuah|salvation}} in Israel.”
Then Samuel said to the people, “Come, let us go to Gilgal and renew the kingship there.”
So all the people went to Gilgal and confirmed Saul as king in the presence of the LORD. There they sacrificed peace offerings before the LORD, and Saul and all the Israelites rejoiced greatly.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, wind, breath. The Spirit of God rushes upon Saul as He did upon the judges, empowering him to rally Israel and deliver Jabesh. The same Spirit that came upon him at his anointing now moves him to act; the whole rescue is presented as the fruit of God's Spirit working through the king, not merely Saul's own resolve.

pachad.gloss: Dread, terror. The terror of the LORD, not merely fear of Saul, falls on the people so that they rally as one man. It is God who moves the nation to respond; the phrase credits the sudden unity and courage of Israel to the LORD, who stirs His people to the deliverance He is about to work.

teshuah.gloss: Salvation, deliverance, victory. Saul refuses revenge because today the LORD has worked teshuah in Israel; he names the rescue as God's saving act, not his own triumph. The word runs through Scripture for God's deliverance of His people, reaching its fullness in the salvation wrought by Jesus, whose very name means the LORD saves.

basic.context: Saul's kingship is tested at once. Nahash, king of the Ammonites, besieges the Israelite town of Jabesh-gilead and offers terms so cruel they are meant to humiliate all Israel: surrender, and I will gouge out every right eye. The desperate town sends for help. When the news reaches Saul, freshly returned from plowing, the Spirit of God rushes on him and he is filled with righteous anger. He summons all Israel with a dramatic call, and the terror of the LORD moves the people to come as one. Saul routs the Ammonites and saves Jabesh. When the crowd wants to kill those who had doubted him, Saul refuses, giving God the glory for the salvation. The kingship is joyfully renewed at Gilgal. The question is: what does a king under God look like? Here, at his best, Saul is a Spirit-filled deliverer who points the glory to the LORD.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative showing Saul at his finest, and it must be read in the flow of the book, which will soon show his decline. The rescue is genuinely good and genuinely God's work through the Spirit-empowered king. We should not simply extract Saul as a hero to imitate, nor ignore that this bright beginning makes his later failure all the more tragic. Its enduring truths are that God saves His people through appointed deliverers empowered by His Spirit, and that the right response to victory is humble praise, not revenge.
basic.originalLanguages: The rescue begins when the ruach, the Spirit of God, rushes on Saul as on the judges, moving him to act. The pachad, the terror of the LORD, not merely of Saul, falls on the people so they rally as one man; God Himself stirs the nation. And Saul names the outcome teshuah, salvation, the LORD has worked salvation in Israel, crediting the deliverance to God and refusing personal revenge.
basic.typology: Saul, empowered by the Spirit to deliver his people and then giving glory to God who worked salvation, is a shadow of the true Deliverer, the Spirit-anointed King who saves His people and gives all glory to the Father. The rescue of a doomed town under threat of blinding mutilation points to the greater salvation of a people doomed under the enemy, accomplished by Christ. Saul's mercy to his critics faintly reflects the King who prays for His enemies.
basic.christ: Saul says, today the LORD has worked salvation in Israel, and in that moment he is a picture of kingship as it should be, God saving His people through His anointed. But Saul's salvation is partial and passing, from one enemy on one day. Jesus, whose name means the LORD saves, is the true and final King who works salvation, not from Ammonites but from sin, death, and the devil, and not for a day but forever. Where Saul spared his critics in an hour of triumph, Christ, in His hour, prayed, Father, forgive them, and saved even His enemies. Saul at his best points to the Saviour who never fails.
basic.god: God is the true worker of salvation, moving through His Spirit-empowered king and stirring His people with holy dread to accomplish rescue. He is not distant from the suffering of a besieged town but raises up a deliverer in answer to their cry. He is the giver of victory, to whom the honor belongs. And He is patient, granting Israel a real deliverance through the king they demanded, confirming the throne in mercy.
basic.crossReferences: Judges 3 and 6 show the Spirit rushing on judges to deliver Israel, the pattern Saul follows. Exodus 14 declares that the salvation is the LORD's. Luke 23 records Christ praying for His enemies. And Matthew 1 gives the name Jesus, for He will save His people from their sins.
basic.oneStory: At his best, Saul is a Spirit-empowered king who saves his people and gives God the glory, showing what kingship under God can be. Yet his salvation is partial and his glory brief. The pattern points beyond him to Christ, the Spirit-anointed King whose name is salvation, who saves His people from their true enemies forever and gives all glory to God.

context.literary: This chapter completes the account of Saul's rise, chapters 9 to 11, and vindicates the doubters of chapter 10 by showing Saul actually saving Israel. It parallels the judges: a threat, the Spirit's rushing, a rallying of the tribes, and deliverance. The renewal of the kingship at Gilgal, verses 14 to 15, closes the section on a high note before Samuel's farewell in chapter 12.
context.historical: The Ammonites lived east of the Jordan and pressed on Israelite territory there. Gouging out the right eye would cripple a man's ability to fight, since the left eye was covered by the shield, and it would publicly shame all Israel. Cutting up the oxen and sending the pieces was a vivid summons to muster, similar to the grim summons in Judges 19. Jabesh-gilead later returns Saul's kindness by rescuing his body, 1 Samuel 31.
context.canonical: Saul's Spirit-empowered deliverance stands in the line of the judges God raised up to save Israel, and it develops the book's theme of God saving through His anointed. Naming the victory as the LORD's salvation anticipates the greater salvation of the gospel, and the true King, Jesus, whose name declares that the LORD saves His people from their sins.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To vindicate Saul's kingship by showing the Spirit-empowered king actually delivering Israel, to credit the salvation to the LORD, and to model humble, God-glorifying leadership before Saul's later decline.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Saul's Spirit-driven rescue and his mercy to critics; it does not prescribe cutting up oxen or waging war as a pattern for believers. What endures is that God works salvation through those He empowers, that victory belongs to the LORD, and that grace, not vengeance, befits the day of God's deliverance.

debates.question: How should we regard Saul in this chapter, given his later failure?

views.label: A genuine high point of Spirit-empowered kingship
views.case: The Spirit really rushed on Saul, God gave a true victory, and Saul rightly credited the LORD and showed mercy; this is authentic good, kingship as God intended, and should be honored as such even in light of what follows.

views.label: A bright beginning that heightens the coming tragedy
views.case: The narrative sets Saul's finest hour early so that his later disobedience and rejection appear all the more sorrowful; the contrast teaches that a gifted, Spirit-touched start is no guarantee of a faithful finish.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: we can honor the real good God did through Saul while learning that empowered beginnings must be matched by persevering obedience, a warning fulfilled positively only in Christ.

debates.question: Why does Saul refuse to punish those who had despised him?

views.label: Humble deflection of glory to God
views.case: Saul insists that today the LORD has worked salvation, so the day belongs to God's mercy, not to settling personal scores; he shows the right instinct that God's deliverance should produce gratitude, not vengeance.

views.label: Political wisdom that unites the nation
views.case: By sparing his critics at the height of his popularity, Saul unites Israel around the throne and the renewal at Gilgal, a magnanimity that consolidates the kingdom without bloodshed.

debates.bearing: Either way Saul's restraint models grace in the hour of triumph, faintly reflecting the greater King who, in His victory, forgives His enemies and saves them.

study.originalLanguages: The whole rescue is framed as God's work through three key words. First, the ruach, the Spirit of God, rushes upon Saul when he hears of Jabesh's plight, exactly as the Spirit came upon the judges to empower them; the deliverance begins not with Saul's ambition but with God's Spirit. Second, when Saul summons the tribes, it is the pachad, the terror, of the LORD, not merely fear of Saul, that falls on the people, so that they come out as one man; the narrator credits Israel's sudden unity and courage to God Himself. Third, when the battle is won and the crowd calls for the blood of Saul's former critics, Saul names the day with the word teshuah, salvation, the LORD has worked salvation in Israel. He refuses to make it about himself, crediting the rescue to God, and that word for God's saving deliverance runs on through the Scriptures to its fullness in Jesus, whose very name means the LORD saves. Spirit, terror, salvation, the vocabulary preaches that this victory is God's.

typology.type: The Spirit-empowered king who saves his people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the Spirit-anointed King who saves His people and gives all glory to the Father, Luke 4:18 and John 17:4.

typology.type: The rescue of the doomed under threat of the enemy
typology.fulfillment: Christ's deliverance of a people doomed under sin, death, and the devil, Colossians 1:13-14.

typology.type: The victorious king who spares his enemies
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who in His triumph prays for and saves His enemies, Luke 23:34 and Romans 5:10.

study.christ: In his finest hour Saul declares, today the LORD has worked salvation in Israel, and for a moment he shows kingship as God meant it to be: the anointed king, filled with the Spirit, saving God's people and giving the glory to God. Yet Saul's salvation is partial and passing, a rescue from one enemy on one day. Jesus is the true and final King, whose very name means the LORD saves, Matthew 1:21, and who works a salvation not from Ammonites but from sin, death, and the devil, and not for a single day but forever, Hebrews 7:25. Where Saul, in his triumph, spared the critics who had despised him, Christ, in His hour of apparent defeat and true victory, prayed, Father, forgive them, and by His death saved even His enemies, Romans 5:10. Saul at his best is a bright but flickering signpost to the Saviour King who never fails and whose salvation cannot be undone.
study.god: This chapter reveals God as the true worker of salvation, who does not stand aloof from the cry of a besieged and threatened people but raises up a deliverer and empowers him by His Spirit. He stirs a whole nation with the terror of the LORD, moving Israel to rally as one, so that the courage and unity of the people are traced to Him. He gives the victory, and the honor for it belongs to Him, as Saul rightly confesses. And He is patient and gracious, granting Israel a real and joyful deliverance through the very king they had demanded, confirming the throne in mercy rather than judgment. The God who saved Jabesh is the God who works salvation still, and whose saving arm reaches its fullness in the gospel of His Son.

crossReferences.note: The Spirit of the LORD clothed Gideon to deliver Israel, the same pattern of Spirit-empowered rescue seen in Saul.
Stand firm and see the salvation of the LORD; the deliverance belongs to God, as Saul confesses of Jabesh.
Father, forgive them; Christ in His hour of victory prays for His enemies, as Saul spared his critics.
You shall call His name Jesus, for He will save His people from their sins, the true and lasting salvation.

study.oneStory: At his best, Saul is a Spirit-empowered king who saves his people from a cruel enemy and gives all the glory to the LORD who worked salvation. It is a glimpse of what kingship under God was meant to be, and it confirms the throne in joy at Gilgal. Yet Saul's salvation is partial and his shining hour brief. The pattern points beyond him to Christ, the Spirit-anointed King whose name is salvation, who saves His people from their true enemies forever, forgives His foes, and gives all glory to God.

quiz.q: What cruel condition did Nahash the Ammonite demand of Jabesh-gilead?

options.text: To gouge out every man's right eye, bringing reproach on all Israel.
options.note: Verse 2; the humiliating terms were meant to shame the whole nation.

options.text: That the men of Jabesh become his servants.
options.note: They offered to serve, but Nahash's added condition was to put out every right eye.

options.text: That Jabesh pay him a yearly tribute of gold.
options.note: The demand was the mutilation of every right eye, not tribute.

options.text: That Jabesh give him their finest soldiers.
options.note: His condition was to blind them in one eye, not conscript soldiers.

quiz.q: What moved Saul to rescue Jabesh-gilead?

options.text: The Spirit of God rushed upon him, and he rallied Israel to deliver the city.
options.note: Verse 6; the deliverance began with the Spirit empowering the king, as with the judges.

options.text: Saul burned with anger when he heard of the threat.
options.note: True, but the anger itself followed the Spirit of God rushing upon him.

options.text: Samuel commanded Saul to go to war.
options.note: The Spirit moved Saul directly; Samuel is summoned to march, not the initiator here.

options.text: Saul wanted to prove himself to those who doubted him.
options.note: The text credits the Spirit of God and, afterward, Saul deflects glory to the LORD.

quiz.q: How did Saul respond when the people wanted to kill those who had doubted him?

options.text: He refused, saying no one should die, for that day the LORD had worked salvation in Israel.
options.note: Verse 13; Saul gave God the glory and showed mercy instead of vengeance.

options.text: He spared the men rather than putting them to death.
options.note: True, but omits his reason, that the LORD had worked the salvation, which is the heart of it.

options.text: He agreed to have the doubters executed.
options.note: The opposite; Saul refused to allow any executions.

options.text: He ignored the people and said nothing.
options.note: He spoke clearly, forbidding any deaths and crediting the LORD.

quiz.q: Who does the chapter credit for the deliverance of Jabesh?

options.text: The LORD, who empowered Saul by His Spirit and worked the salvation.
options.note: The Spirit, the terror of the LORD, and Saul's own words all credit God.

options.text: Saul, whose leadership organized and won the battle.
options.note: Saul led, but the text and Saul himself credit the LORD for the salvation.

options.text: The 300,000 soldiers of Israel who fought bravely.
options.note: The army fought, but the deliverance is attributed to God, who rallied and empowered them.

options.text: The elders of Jabesh, who devised a clever plan.
options.note: The rescue came from God through Saul, not from a plan of the elders.

quiz.q: How does Saul's deliverance of Jabesh point forward to Christ?

options.text: As a Spirit-empowered king who works salvation and spares his enemies, he foreshadows Christ, whose name means the LORD saves.
options.note: Saul's partial, passing salvation points to Christ's full and lasting salvation, Matthew 1:21.

options.text: He shows that God rescues His people through leaders He empowers.
options.note: True, but stops short of Christ, the true saving King the chapter anticipates.

options.text: He proves that a good beginning guarantees a faithful life.
options.note: Saul's later failure shows the opposite; only Christ perfectly perseveres.

options.text: He has no connection to Christ.
options.note: The Spirit-empowered saving king clearly foreshadows Jesus.

questions: The rescue of Jabesh began when the Spirit of God moved Saul to act. Where is God stirring you to step into a need rather than staying behind your own plow?
Saul gave God the glory, saying the LORD has worked salvation. When something goes well for you, is your first instinct to credit God or to take the praise yourself?
In his hour of triumph Saul spared those who had despised him. Is there someone who wronged or doubted you whom God is calling you to forgive rather than repay?
Israel rallied when the terror of the LORD fell on them. What would it look like for a right and holy fear of God to move you and your community to action?
Saul's salvation was real but partial; Christ's is complete and lasting. How does resting in the finished salvation of Jesus free you from fear of your true enemies?

translationNotes.note: The BSB notes that the Dead Sea Scrolls and Septuagint read About a month later Nahash, and a longer scroll passage gives background to the siege; the main text follows the shorter Hebrew reading.
The terror of the LORD, pachad, fell on the people, crediting Israel's swift, united response to God, not merely to fear of Saul's grim summons.
The numbers 300,000 and 30,000 follow the Hebrew; the BSB footnote notes the Dead Sea Scrolls and Septuagint read 70,000 men of Judah.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 1 Samuel 11:1-15. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced anywhere in the file. Sermon status none: no sermon from the trusted cluster verified this session for this passage.
